Object Detection Using YOLOv4 with a GUI-Based Interactive 
System 
Deepak N. A.1*, Abhishek Gangappa Ambi2, Shree Sagar CV3, Sai Koushik YR4, Siddaraju T 5  
 
1Associate Professor, Computer Science and Engineering, RV Institute of Technology 
and Management (RVITM), Bengaluru, India, Email: deepakna.rvitm@rvei.edu.in. 
 
2,3,4,5 UG Students, Computer Science and Engineering, RV Institute of Technology 
and Management (RVITM), Bengaluru, India. 
 
Abstract

Abstract 
Object detection is a fundamental area of contemporary computer vision, allowing machines to detect, 
classify, and locate objects in visual scenes. This project demonstrates the implementation of a GUI-
based real-time Object Detection system based on the YOLOv4 model, combined with OpenCV and 
Python's Tkinter library. The system enables users to load static images, browse through image datasets,

and capture live video from a webcam for real-time detection. The main features are zooming, dragging, 
dynamic resizing of the image, and interactive control of the camera output. By integrating an efficient 
detection model with an intuitive graphical user interface, the program closes the gap between cutting-
edge deep learning technology and accessible real-world equipment. This system illustrates the

significance of model deployment, human interaction, and real-time feedback in real-world AI-based 
applications. 
Keywords: Object Detection, YOLOv4, Real-Time Computer Vision, OpenCV, GUI, Tkinter,  
Deep Learning, Image Processing, Bounding Box Detection 
 
1. Introduction 
The domain of computer vision has grown tremendously, allowing machines to sense and interpret the 
visual world with greater precision. One such key computer vision task, object detection, is instrumental

in numerous applications such as autonomous vehicles, surveillance, industrial automation, and 
infrastructure for smart cities. It is not just detecting objects appearing in an image but also localizing 
them precisely with bounding boxes. 
Deep learning frameworks, especially Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), have completely 
transformed the domain of object detection. Out of these, YOLO (You Only Look Once) suite of

algorithms was remarkable in both achieving real-time speed at preserving high precision. YOLOv4 
released in 2020 by Bochkovskiy et al. achieved the highest jump yet from the predecessors by

implementing a chain of enhancements like Cross-Stage Partial connections (CSP), Spatial Pyramid 
Pooling (SPP), and Self-Adversarial Training (SAT). 
In spite of the advancements made in detection precision and speed, real-world utilization tends to be a 
seamless, user-friendly, and accessible interface that enables even non-experts to use such high-powered 
models with ease. To fill the gap, the current project envisages a GUI-based Object Detection system

that marries YOLOv4 with Python's Tkinter and OpenCV frameworks. The software enables users to 
load images or take live video from a camera, detect objects automatically, and browse images 
interactively with zoom, drag, and real-time updates. 
This work not only illustrates the deployment of real-time object detection but also highlights the 
significance of successful model deployment in real-world, user-oriented applications. 
2. Literature Review

2. Literature Review 
Recent advancements in deep learning have seen considerable improvements in object detection models. 
Bochkovskiy et al. (2020) introduced YOLOv4, incorporating features like CSPNet, Mish activation, 
and Cross mini-Batch Normalization (CmBN), achieving state-of-the-art performance on the COCO 
benchmark. Wang et al. (2021) extended this work by proposing Scaled-YOLOv4, enabling multi-scale 
training to improve performance on datasets with variable object sizes.

Kumar and Safwan (2024) addressed the adaptation of YOLOv4 for real-time environments, 
emphasizing optimization for dynamic and resource-constrained settings. Ji et al. (2022) developed 
lightweight YOLOv4 models for safety equipment detection tasks, combining YOLOv4 with MobileNet 
to balance performance and computational efficiency. 
In addition, Dubey et al. (2022) applied YOLOv4 modifications to satellite imagery, showing that

YOLO-based architectures can be adapted to different environmental challenges with minor alterations. 
Comparative studies such as those by Zhaosheng Xu et al. (2023) also indicate that traditional deep-
learning-based models like YOLOv4 still outperform newer lightweight models in terms of reliability 
and precision when configured properly. 
These research contributions strongly motivate the integration of YOLOv4 into accessible, deployable

systems, where users can harness its capabilities without deep technical expertise. Our work follows this 
direction by embedding YOLOv4 in a dynamic, user-centric GUI system. 
3. Research Methodology 
This project follows a systematic approach to integrate object detection capabilities into an interactive, 
real-time system, divided into the following stages:

A. Image Acquisition and Camera Integration 
The system allows users to either load static images using a file dialog or capture live video feeds from 
the system’s webcam through OpenCV’s VideoCapture() function. 
B. Preprocessing 
Captured frames or loaded images are resized and normalized into a blob using OpenCV's 
blobFromImage() function. This ensures compatibility with YOLOv4’s input specifications. 
C. Object Detection Using YOLOv4

C. Object Detection Using YOLOv4 
The preprocessed frames are passed into the YOLOv4 deep neural network. Output layers provide 
bounding boxes, class labels, and confidence scores. Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) is applied to 
filter overlapping detections. 
D. Visualization and GUI Operations 
Using Tkinter’s canvas, the application displays the detection results in real-time. Features include: 
 
Zooming and Dragging: Users can zoom into detected objects or drag images within the 
window.

window. 
 
Navigation: Users can switch between loaded images using "Next" and "Previous" buttons. 
 
Camera Controls: Start/Stop camera feed dynamically. 
 
Dynamic Updates: The GUI automatically refreshes every 10ms when the camera is active. 
E. Evaluation and Testing 
The system’s responsiveness, detection accuracy, and usability were evaluated across various input 
conditions, including lighting variations, occlusions, and multiple object scenarios.

Results and Discussion 
The YOLOv4-based Object Detection GUI application developed effectively combines real-time 
detection capabilities into an easy-to-use and accessible interface. The system was tested with a variety 
of static images and dynamic live feeds to assess both performance and usability. 
The object detection module was able to process images at real-time rates effectively, spotting multiple 
objects in diverse lighting conditions and busy backgrounds. Application of Non-Maximum

Suppression (NMS) helped effectively reduce redundant detections, which led to neat bounding box 
visualizations. The Tkinter-based user interface remained highly responsive to user actions such as 
zooming, dragging, and image switching, even when the live camera feed was turned on. 
Dynamic image resizing and real-time updating of frames enabled users to watch the detection process 
seamlessly without suffering from GUI delay. The software also exhibited stability over extended live

video durations, ensuring synchronization among the camera stream and detection results. 
Quantitative Analysis 
The quantitative evaluation of the developed system focused on measuring the object detection accuracy, frame 
processing speed, and the responsiveness of the graphical interface. Using a dataset of 50 images containing 
diverse object categories such as people, vehicles, and animals, the YOLOv4 model achieved a mean Average

Precision (mAP) of approximately 45–50% at a confidence threshold of 0.5, which is consistent with real-world 
performance benchmarks. During live video streaming via webcam, the system maintained an operational frame 
rate of 18–25 frames per second (FPS) on a standard CPU-based laptop, indicating that real-time detection is 
feasible even without dedicated GPU acceleration. In static image mode, object detection was performed at an

average time of 0.15 to 0.25 seconds per image, depending on the input resolution. Navigation operations such as 
switching between images were fast, with an average response time of less than 0.1 seconds. Furthermore, 
interactive functionalities like zooming and dragging exhibited minimal impact on system performance, with less 
than a 5% drop in frame rate during active user interaction. These results affirm the effectiveness and practical

efficiency of the developed object detection system. 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Fig 1.3 Object Distribution for input sports image

Fig 1.4 Object Distribution for input Traffic image 
Qualitative Analysis 
Qualitative analysis was conducted to assess the usability, clarity of output, and overall user experience of the 
system. The bounding boxes generated by YOLOv4 accurately localized objects within both static images and 
dynamic video feeds, providing clear and visually distinct object labels even in complex backgrounds. The

Tkinter-based GUI was evaluated by users with varying levels of technical expertise, who reported that the 
interface was intuitive, easy to navigate, and responsive.  
Real-time feedback during camera streaming allowed users to observe the detection process interactively, 
enhancing engagement and understanding of detection dynamics. Visual elements, such as bounding boxes and 
color-coded labels, improved interpretability without cluttering the display.

The system demonstrated robust performance during prolonged operation, with no significant memory leaks or 
crashes observed even after extended sessions. However, some limitations were noted under low-light conditions, 
where detection confidence slightly decreased, and during extreme zooming where pixelation artifacts appeared 
without majorly affecting detection accuracy. Overall, the system provided a seamless and satisfying user
experience, validating its design objectives for accessibility, functionality, and real-time interaction.

Fig 1.6 Live testing of Object detection

Conclusion 
In this project, we built a GUI for object detection that used advanced machine learning algorithms to 
detect and classify objects in images and video streams in real-time. The system was efficient and user-
friendly, with an easy-to-use interface that permitted users to upload images or video feeds and view 
detection results instantly, including bounding boxes, labels, and confidence scores. The object

detection model showed good performance, correctly identifying objects in varied conditions, such as 
different lighting and object orientations. The real-time processing ability was crucial for instant 
feedback and to improve the user experience as a whole. 
There were some issues though, such as incorrect positives and negatives, model restriction in intricate 
environments, and resource usage limitations during processing, especially for more demanding

hardware. These problems indicated the necessity for further optimization in the underlying model and 
the GUI design. In the future, improvements will be made to improve the detection accuracy with more 
sophisticated algorithms and optimize the GUI for wider applications. This may include fine-tuning the 
system for edge and mobile devices, adding support for features such as multi-object tracking, and

broadening the application of the system in different industries including security, healthcare, and retail. 
The project overall was able to create a working and useful object detection tool, and with ongoing 
improvements, it has tremendous potential for real-world use across a wide range of areas.

Reference
